elements.sent-2411-003: Petropulu is with the Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Rutgers, The State University of New Jersey, Piscataway, NJ 08854 USA (email: athinap@soe.rutgers.edu).
elements.sent-2411-004: This paper presents an off-the-grid estimator for ISAC systems using lifted atomic norm minimization (LANM).
elements.sent-2411-005: The main challenge in the ISAC systems is the unknown nature of both transmitted signals and radar-communication channels.
elements.sent-2411-006: We use a known dictionary to encode transmit signals and show that LANM can localize radar targets and decode communication symbols when the number of observations is proportional to the system's degrees of freedom and the coherence of the dictionary matrix.
elements.sent-2411-007: We reformulate LANM using a dual method and solve it with semidefinite relaxation (SDR) for different dictionary matrices to reduce the number of observations required at the receiver.
elements.sent-2411-008: Simulations demonstrate that the proposed LANM accurately estimates communication data and target parameters under varying complexity by selecting different dictionary matrices.
elements.sent-2411-009: Recent interest in communication and radar spectrum sharing (CRSS) has led to two main approaches: radar-communication coexistence and integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) systems \cite{liu2020joint, meng2023network .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Radar-communication coexistence develops interference management strategies, allowing both systems to operate without disrupting each other \cite{vargas2023dual .}
elements.sent-2411-011: ISAC integrates sensing and communication within the same system, providing real-time cooperation \cite{valiulahi2023net,alaaeldin2023robust,valiulahi2022antenna,virgili2022cost .}
elements.sent-2411-012: ISAC systems not only boost spectral and energy efficiency but also lower hardware and signaling costs by combining both functions \cite{liu2022integrated, hua1993pencil .}
elements.sent-2411-013: These advantages have expanded ISAC's applications to fields such as vehicular networks, indoor positioning, and covert communications \cite{meng2023sensing, meng2024cooperative, blunt2010intrapulse .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Unlike traditional approaches that consider sensing and communication as separate systems, ISAC mutually designs them for mutual benefits, maximizing resource use and operational effectiveness \cite{liu2018toward,han2024beamspace .}
elements.sent-2411-015: The main challenge in ISAC reception is that both transmit signals and channels are unknown, making traditional radar and communication methods ineffective.
elements.sent-2411-016: ISAC receivers estimate both target parameters and communication data simultaneously \cite{liu2020joint,hu2024isac .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Techniques like MUSIC \cite{schmidt1986multiple and ESPRIT \cite{yan2019two struggle with noise and correlated targets, while compressed sensing (CS) improves resolution but assumes grid-based DOAs, leading to errors \cite{han2022high, chi2011sensitivity .}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: These methods also fail to jointly detect DOAs and communication data.
elements.sent-2411-019: To address basis mismatch, atomic norm minimization (ANM) was developed, promoting signal sparsity in a continuous dictionary, serving as a continuous version of $\ell_{1}$ norm minimization.
elements.sent-2411-020: ANM has been applied in MIMO radar \cite{tang2020range , line spectral estimation \cite{valiulahi2019robustness , and OFDM noise elimination \cite{valiulahi2019eliminating , outperforming conventional methods.}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: It has also been used for delay-Doppler and angle-delay-Doppler pair recovery \cite{heckel2016super, heckel2016super1 .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Lifted ANM (LANM) handles blind detection in super-resolution problems by estimating both spectral coefficients and transmit signals \cite{chi2016guaranteed \cite{bigdeli2022noncoherent , addressing AWGN and differentiating radar from communication signals in spectrum coexistence \cite{suliman2021mathematical, vargas2023dual .}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: We propose a novel ISAC receiver design based on LANM that can simultaneously recover target locations, velocities, delays, DOAs, and communication data from reflected signals.
elements.sent-2411-024: Despite the previous developments on the LANM in one dimensional signaling, we introduce a theorem that adapts the LANM technique for MIMO scenarios in ISAC systems.
elements.sent-2411-025: Consider a bistatic radar scenario as shown in Fig. \ref{fig:drawing1 .}
elements.sent-2411-026: The transmitter illuminates signals while the receiver collects echoes from $K$ targets.
elements.sent-2411-027: The challenge is to simultaneously detect the target parameters and estimate the transmitted signals with communication data.
elements.sent-2411-028: This problem is bilinear and difficult to solve.
elements.sent-2411-029: Traditionally, it is assumed that a reference link or pilot signals are used, which wastes bandwidth.
elements.sent-2411-030: The estimated signal may have demodulation errors, leading to reduced performance \cite{zheng2017super .}
elements.sent-2411-031: Additionally, physical obstructions might block the radio waves, making the direct link unavailable.
elements.sent-2411-032: We propose a LANM-based approach, assuming transmitted signals lie on a known low-dimensional random dictionary.
elements.sent-2411-033: The received signal is modeled as a sparse combination of low-rank matrices, addressed using LANM.
elements.sent-2411-034: We derive a dual formulation to ensure exact solutions under certain conditions and prove that the number of samples required for accurate estimation is proportional to the number of targets, transmit symbols, and the coherence of the compression matrix.
elements.sent-2411-035: Different compression matrices lead to varying receiver complexity, allowing us to reduce implementation complexity by either reducing the number of antennas or lowering the sampling rate.
elements.sent-2411-036: Simulations confirm that our estimator accurately detects both communication data and target parameters with adjustable complexity at the receiver.
elements.sent-2411-037: Here, we introduce the notation used in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-038: Vectors and matrices are denoted by boldface lowercase and uppercase letters, respectively and scalars or entries are non-bold lowercase.
elements.sent-2411-039: The $\|\cdot\|_1$ and $\|\cdot\|_2$ are $\ell_1$ and $\ell_2$ norms, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-040: The operators $\mathrm{tr (\cdot)$ and $(\cdot)^H$ are trace of a matrix, hermitian of a vector, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-041: We consider an ISAC system with a transmitter having $N_t$ antennas and a receiver with $N_r$ antennas, targeting $K$ objects, where $K < N_t$.
elements.sent-2411-042: The targets are located in the far field of the arrays, with uniformly spaced transmit and receive antennas, where the spacing is $\frac{N_t {2f_c}$ and $\frac{N_r {2f_c}$, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-043: The baseband received signal $y_r(t)$ at the $r$-th receive antenna, $r = \{0, \cdots, N_r-1\}$, is the sum of reflected signals from the targets, denoted by $x_k(t)$ for $k = \{0, \cdots, K-1\}$.
elements.sent-2411-044: Mathematically, this can be expressed as: \begin{equation}\label{1} y_r(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{K-1} \sum_{s=0}^{N_t-1} \alpha_k e^{i2\pi r N_t \phi_k} e^{i2\pi s \theta_k} x_k(t - \bar{\tau}_k) e^{i2\pi \bar{v}_k t},\end{equation} where $i=\sqrt{-1}$, $\alpha_k \in \mathbb{C}$ is the complex attenuation factor representing radar path loss and cross-section, $\theta_k = -\frac{\sin(\tilde{\theta _k) {2}$ and $\phi_k = -\frac{\sin(\tilde{\phi _k) {2}$ are the angle of departure (AoD) and angle of arrival (AoA) for the $k$-th target, respectively, as shown in Fig. \ref{fig:drawing1 .}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\label{1} y_r(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{K-1} \sum_{s=0}^{N_t-1} \alpha_k e^{i2\pi r N_t \phi_k} e^{i2\pi s \theta_k} x_k(t - \bar{\tau}_k) e^{i2\pi \bar{v}_k t},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-045: These parameters $\theta_k, \phi_k, \bar{\tau _k, \bar{v _k$ can be translated into the angle, distance, and velocity of each target relative to the radar.}}
elements.sent-2411-046: Our goal is to recover the parameters $\alpha_k, \theta_k, \phi_k, \bar{\tau _k, \bar{v _k$ from the received signal $y_r(t)$ and estimate the probing signals $x_k(t)$ containing communication data.}}
elements.sent-2411-047: There is no assumed direct transmitter-receiver link, as this could waste bandwidth and introduce demodulation errors \cite{zheng2017super , and obstacles may block direct paths.}
elements.sent-2411-048: The transmit signal is assumed to be band- and time-limited, and the received signal is sampled at a rate of $\frac{1 {B}$ over the interval $[0, T_t]$, where the samples are collected into a vector $\bm{y _r \in \mathbb{C ^{\bar{L }$, with $\bar{L := BT$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: The $p$-th entry of $\bm{y _r$ is given by: \begin{equation}\label{2} [y_r]_p = \frac{1}{\bar{L}} \sum_{k=0}^{K-1} \sum_{s=0}^{N_t-1} |\alpha_k| e^{i2\pi r N_t \phi_k} e^{i2\pi (s \theta_k +\theta^{\alpha}_{k})} \nonumber \sum_{r=-N}^{N} \Bigg[\Bigg( \sum_{l=-N}^{N} x_k(l) e^{-i2\pi\frac{rl}{\bar{L}}} \Bigg) e^{-i2\pi p \tau_k} \Bigg] e^{i2\pi \frac{rp}{\bar{L}}} e^{i2\pi v p},\end{equation} where $\tau_k = \frac{\bar{\tau _k {T}$ and $v_k = \frac{\bar{v _k {B}$ are the normalized time and frequency shifts, respectively.}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\label{2} [y_r]_p = \frac{1}{\bar{L}} \sum_{k=0}^{K-1} \sum_{s=0}^{N_t-1} |\alpha_k| e^{i2\pi r N_t \phi_k} e^{i2\pi (s \theta_k +\theta^{\alpha}_{k})} \nonumber \sum_{r=-N}^{N} \Bigg[\Bigg( \sum_{l=-N}^{N} x_k(l) e^{-i2\pi\frac{rl}{\bar{L}}} \Bigg) e^{-i2\pi p \tau_k} \Bigg] e^{i2\pi \frac{rp}{\bar{L}}} e^{i2\pi v p},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-051: The goal is now to estimate the locations of the targets, which requires determining the parameters $\alpha_k$, $(\theta_k, \phi_k, \tau_k, v_k) \in [0,1)^4$, and recovering the probing signal $\bm{x _k$, $k = \{0, \cdots, K-1\}$ that contains communication data, from the observation model in (\ref{2 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-052: The number of unknowns in (\ref{2 ) is in order of $\mathcal{O (\bar{L S)$, far exceeding the number of observations, making the problem ill-posed.}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: To address this, we assume the probing signals $\bm{x _k \in \mathbb{C ^{\bar{L \times 1 , k = \{0, \cdots, K-1\}$, lie in a known low-dimensional subspace, represented by a matrix $\bm{D \in \mathbb{C ^{\bar{L \times T}$ with $T \ll \bar{L}$, which we call it the compression matrix.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Hence, $\bm{x _k = \bm{D \bm{h _k$, where $\bm{h _k \in \mathbb{C ^{T \times 1}$ is an unknown vector carrying the communication data.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: Recovering $\bm{x _k$ reduces to estimating $\bm{h _k$, as $\bm{D}$ is known by both transmitter and receiver.}}
elements.sent-2411-056: Without loss of generality, we assume that $\|\bm{h _{k}\|_{2 = 1$ for all $k$.}}
elements.sent-2411-057: Note that recovering $\bm{x _{k}$ is equivalent to estimating $\bm{h _{k}$ since $\bm{D}$ is known by both the transmitter and receiver.}}
elements.sent-2411-059: The array response $\bm{a (\bm{\tau _k)$ is defined as: \begin{equation}[a(\tau_k)]_{(r, s, l, k, 1)} = e^{i2\pi r N_t \phi_k} e^{i2\pi (s \theta_k+\theta_{k}^{\alpha})} D_N \bigg(\frac{l}{L}-\tau_k\bigg) \nonumber D_N\bigg(\frac{r}{L}-v_k\bigg),\end{equation} where $r \in \{0, \cdots, N_r-1\}$, $s \in \{0, \cdots, N_t-1\}$, $l,k \in \{-N, \cdots, N\}$, and $D_N(t)$ is the Dirichlet kernel.}}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}[a(\tau_k)]_{(r, s, l, k, 1)} = e^{i2\pi r N_t \phi_k} e^{i2\pi (s \theta_k+\theta_{k}^{\alpha})} D_N \bigg(\frac{l}{L}-\tau_k\bigg) \nonumber D_N\bigg(\frac{r}{L}-v_k\bigg),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-060: Using this, we rewrite (\ref{2 ) as: \begin{equation}[y]_j = \sum_{k=0}^{K-1} \alpha_k [a(\tau_k)]_{(r,s,l,k,1)} d_{(j-l)}^H h_k e^{i2\pi \frac{pr}{L}}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-061: With the linear operator $\mathcal{X}$ and its adjoint $\mathcal{X ^\star$, then, we can write \begin{equation}\label{eq9} y = X(U).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Equation (\ref{eq9 ) shows that recovery depends on $\bm{U}$, which is a sparse combination of matrices from the set of atoms \begin{equation}A = \{va(\tau)^{H} \mid \tau \in [0,1)^{4}, \|v\|_2 = 1\}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-063: The atomic norm of $\bm{U}$ is defined as the gauge function of $\mathrm{conv (\mathcal{A )$: \begin{equation}\|U\|_{A} = \inf\Big\{\sum_k \alpha_k \mid U = \sum_{k=0}^{K-1} \alpha_k h_k a(\tau_k)^{H}, \alpha_k \geq 0 \Big\}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-064: To estimate $\mathcal{X (\bm{U )$ from (\ref{eq9 ), we propose minimizing the atomic norm: \begin{equation}\min_{U}~~ \|U\|_{A}\nonumber s.t.~y=X(U),\end{equation} Solving this is difficult, so we consider its dual as below \begin{equation}\max_{q} ~\langleq,y_{w}\rangle_{R} \quad s.t. \quad \|X^{\star}(q)\|_{A}^{\star} \leq 1,\end{equation} where $\bm{q}$ is the dual variable and $\|\cdot\|_{\mathcal{A ^{\star}$ is the dual atomic norm.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-065: In the following, we present the main results and theorem.
elements.sent-2411-066: We also assume $\mu T\geq 1$ for simplicity, achievable by selecting a sufficiently large $\mu$.
elements.sent-2411-067: Normalizing $\bm{h _{k}$ still conveys QAM information, as the receiver knows the modulation scheme and signal size.}
elements.sent-2411-068: Regarding the above discussion, we now present our main theorem as follows: \begin{thm Theorem \ref{maintheorem states that for the linear system in (\ref{1 ) and its sampled version in (\ref{2 ), if the unknown waveforms can be expressed as $\bm{x _{k = \bm{D}\bm{h _{k}$, where $\bm{D}$ satisfies Assumption \ref{assum1 and $\bm{h _{k}$ follows Assumption \ref{assumption2 , and if the shifts meet the minimum separation in Assumption \ref{assumption3 , then with probability at least $1 - \delta$, the condition $ L^4 \geq C\mu KT \log\left(\frac{10KT {\delta}\right) $ ensures that $\bm{U}$ can be recovered through problem (\ref{eq9 ).}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: This result shows that, with enough radar samples relative to a logarithmic factor, an exact solution exists under mild separation conditions.
elements.sent-2411-070: Thus, we consider the following observation model \begin{equation}y_{w}=X(U)+w,\end{equation} containing both AWGN with the power $\|\bm{w}\|_{2 \le \sigma^{2}$.}

elements.sent-2411-076: This convex problem can be solved with tools like CVX \cite{grant2014cvx .The transmitted data can be obtained by solving the following optimization problem: \begin{equation}\label{12} \min_{g_{k}, \forall k, p_{r}p_{r}, \forall r}\sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{L} \bigg([y_{w}]_{j}-\sum_{k =0}^{K-1}a(\hat{\tau}_{k})^{H}\tilde{D}_{j}g_{k})\bigg)^{2}}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-077: In the following subsections, we introduce various compression matrices, each leading to a different $\mu$, which in turn impacts the required number of samples at the receiver.

elements.sent-2411-116: If the error is less than or equal to $10^{-3}$, the recovery is considered successful.

elements.sent-2411-119: The results across these matrices demonstrate similar trends in both NMSE and SER.
elements.sent-2411-120: In this paper, we proposed a LANM-based estimator for ISAC super-resolution receivers, enabling simultaneous radar target parameter estimation and communication symbol decoding.
elements.sent-2411-121: By employing different compression matrices—Fourier, Hadamard, and Gaussian—we demonstrated that LANM maintains robust performance across varying SNR levels, with similar trends in NMSE and SER across the matrices.
elements.sent-2411-122: The results highlight that increasing the number of measurements improves recovery performance.
elements.sent-2411-123: Our findings confirm LANM's ability to efficiently balance between sensing and communication tasks with flexibility in implementation complexity.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}
